authorship_evidence: Project created in 2014 for PyCon talk, pre-LLM era, consistent human commit history from single author

evidence.description: Path traversal via unsanitized 'p' GET parameter joined directly into file path: open(os.path.join(base_path, filename), 'rb').read(). An attacker can use '../../' sequences to read arbitrary files (e.g., ?p=../../settings.py to read Django settings including SECRET_KEY).

Arbitrary code execution via exec() on user-supplied base64-decoded POST data (Python 3 path): exec(base64.decodestring(bytes(first_name, 'ascii'))). Attacker can execute any Python code on the server.

Arbitrary code execution via exec() on user-supplied base64-decoded POST data (Python 2 fallback path): exec(base64.decodestring(first_name)). Same vulnerability as the Python 3 path but for Python 2 compatibility.

Reflected XSS via |safe filter on user-controlled 'qs' GET parameter in form input value attribute: value="{{qs|safe}}". Attacker can break out of the attribute and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript (e.g., qs=" onfocus=alert(1) autofocus x=").

Reflected XSS via |safe filter on URL path variable injected into JavaScript context: var myvar = "{{path|safe}}";. Attacker can break out of the string and execute arbitrary JavaScript by manipulating the URL path.
Reflected XSS via |safe filter on 'qs' GET parameter injected into JavaScript context: var myvar = "{{qs|safe}}";. Attacker can break out of the string and execute arbitrary JavaScript.

Reflected XSS via |safe filter on 'qs' GET parameter injected into JavaScript context: var x = "{{qs|safe}}";. This XSS is used in the CSRF exercise to demonstrate how XSS enables CSRF attacks via image tag injection.

Open redirect via unsanitized 'url' GET parameter passed directly to Django redirect(): return redirect(url). An attacker can craft a URL like ?url=http://evil.com to redirect users to a malicious site.

Unsafe function dispatch via globals().get(forward) using user-supplied 'fwd' GET parameter. An attacker can call any function in the module's global scope by name (e.g., ?fwd=admin to access the admin view, or ?fwd=boom to trigger errors). This is an unvalidated forward vulnerability.

Missing function-level access control. The admin page is served to anyone who passes ?action=admin in the GET parameter with no authentication or authorization check. Any user can access the admin template by simply adding the query parameter.

Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR). User profiles are accessed and modified by sequential numeric userid in URL (/direct-object-references/users/1, /users/2) with no authentication or authorization checks. Any user can view and modify any other user's profile data by changing the userid parameter.

DEBUG = True in production settings. This exposes detailed stack traces, SQL queries, template context, and settings values to any user who triggers an error. The boom() view at /misconfiguration/boom deliberately raises an exception to demonstrate this information disclosure.

Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in source code: 'h++jszpm)i@p%ay_b=cp#()^od!qns14)h%@qm3)p=cuo+st^a'. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, CSRF tokens, and password reset tokens. Exposure allows session forgery and other attacks.

Global CSRF protection is disabled. The CsrfViewMiddleware is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES: #'django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware'. This means no views have CSRF protection, making all POST forms vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.

View explicitly decorated with @csrf_exempt, additionally disabling any CSRF protection. Combined with the XSS vulnerability on the same page (|safe filter on qs parameter), this enables CSRF attacks via injected image tags to trigger gift card purchases.

Gift card purchase form with no CSRF token: <form method="POST"> without {% csrf_token %}. Combined with the globally disabled CSRF middleware, this form is vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks that could trigger unauthorized gift card purchases.

Django Debug Toolbar is enabled and can be activated by any user via ?debug=1 query parameter, debug POST field, or debug cookie. The toolbar exposes SQL queries, request/response headers, template context, settings, and other sensitive internal application details.

Intentional exception raise Exception('boom') in a view with DEBUG=True. This causes Django to display a full debug error page including the complete stack trace, settings (with SECRET_KEY), environment variables, and installed middleware to any user.
XFrameOptionsMiddleware is commented out: # 'django.middleware.clickjacking.XFrameOptionsMiddleware'. This allows the application to be framed by any origin, enabling clickjacking attacks.

The code_execution.html POST form lacks a {% csrf_token %} tag. With CSRF middleware globally disabled (settings.py), an attacker can forge a cross-origin POST request to the code execution endpoint, which passes submitted data to exec(base64.decode(POST['first_name'])). This enables CSRF-triggered remote code execution. The ground truth already tracks per-form CSRF omissions as distinct entries (e.g. gift_card.html in GT-016), and this form targets the most dangerous endpoint in the application (RCE via exec).

The SQL injection exercise POST form lacks a {% csrf_token %} tag. With CSRF middleware globally disabled in settings.py, this form has no CSRF protection at either the middleware or template layer. An attacker can forge cross-origin POST requests to this endpoint. Even if the middleware were re-enabled, the form would remain unprotected due to the missing token.

The xss_form view sets a cookie via response.set_cookie(key='monster', value='omnomnomnomnom!') without httponly=True. This makes the cookie accessible to JavaScript via document.cookie. Combined with the reflected XSS vulnerability on the same page (GT-004, |safe filter on qs parameter in form.html), an attacker can steal this cookie value through injected JavaScript.

The xss_form view sets a cookie via response.set_cookie(key='monster', value='omnomnomnomnom!') at line 100 without secure=True. The cookie will be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, allowing interception by network attackers. This is a distinct issue from the missing HttpOnly flag (lets-be-bad-guys-022) on the same set_cookie call.

SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE is not set in settings.py (defaults to False). The session engine is configured at line 174 (SESSION_ENGINE = 'django.contrib.sessions.backends.cache') but session cookies will be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, allowing interception by network attackers performing MITM or sniffing. This is a missing security hardening configuration rather than an explicit misconfiguration.

The sql() view contains SQL injection-looking strings ("SELECT id from Users where first_name = ''; DROP TABLE Users;--") but these are only used as display text for the exercise solution. No actual database query is executed — the strings are passed directly to the template for rendering. The view only compares user input against an expected answer string.

The 'fwd' parameter is displayed in {{ fwd }} but Django auto-escapes template variables by default (no |safe filter used). The output is placed inside a <code> tag and will be HTML-escaped, preventing XSS injection.

User input 'name' is rendered in an HTML input value attribute: <input name="name" value="{{ name }}">. While this looks like it could be XSS, Django's default auto-escaping will HTML-encode special characters (<, >, ", &), preventing attribute breakout and XSS injection.

The 'msg' variable is rendered with {{ msg }} in the template, which looks like it could allow XSS. However, the msg variable is set server-side to fixed strings ('That was worth trying...', 'You're on the right track...', 'Keep trying...') and is never directly user-controlled. Django's auto-escaping is also active.
